pirate: Arrr, 700 lb o' timber, give or take a rum barrel!
Arrr! Nigh 700 pounds o' timber, give or take a barnacle's worth o' doubt!
Arrr! If yon whistle-pig could heave timber, she'd chuck nigh on 318 kilos — seven hundred pound o' lumber — in the diggin' of one den, give or take a keg's worth o' doubt. Yo ho!
Arrr, 'bout 700 pounds o' timber, give or take, though the beastie never chucked a splinter!
Arr! The furry landlubber'd chuck nigh on 360 kilos — 800 pounds o' pine — same as the dirt she shovels fer her burrow, give or take a third. Now swab the deck!

> Arrr! If yon burrowin' beastie could heave timber like it heaves dirt, she'd chuck near seven hundred pound o' wood, give or take a plank or three! But mark me words, matey—the scurvy creature chucks naught, on account o' havin' no arms fit fer the throwin'. A fine riddle to shiver yer timbers! ---

haiku: if teeth could hurl the forest,
pirate: Arr! If that scurvy land-marmot could heave timber like it digs its dirt-hole, it'd sling near two-hundred-forty-five stone-weights (that be 'bout five hundred forty pounds, ye landlubbers) o' good oak—give or take a barrel, since nobody rightly knows what "chuckin'" even means. Yo ho! ---
Arr! She'd chuck near 640 pound o' wood a day, give or take a heap. The old salts reckon 700. Close enough!

> Arr! If yon land-marmot could heave timber like it heaves dirt, she'd sling near seven hundred pound o' wood afore grog o'clock — give or take, ye scurvy dogs, for none has ever seen the beast do it, an' none ever shall! Yo ho. ---
Arrr, near seven hunnerd pound o' timber, give or take a swig!

haiku: dirt becomes an oak dream, thrown
pirate: If a woodchuck could chuck wood like it chucks dirt, it'd fling about 700 lb on a good day, but truth be told it chucks no wood at all, only dirt and dreams.
If a woodchuck could chuck wood like she chucks dirt, she'd chuck about forty pounds a day - but she can't, it's all a tall tale, savvy?
Arrr, a woodchuck be chuckin' near seven hundred pounds (320 kilograms) o' wood a day, in legend only, for no woodchuck ever chucks a single splinter, ye scallywag!
Near two hundred pound o' timber, give or take a hull's worth, arrr.
If that burrow-beast could heave timber like it heaves dirt, 'twould chuck about 700 lbs a day, give or take, though no woodchuck's ever truly chucked a plank!

Arr, a woodchuck be chuckin’ nigh on seven tonnes o’ wood a day, if the scurvy beast had a mind to! That be a heap o’ timber—enough to keep the ship’s carpenter busy for a fortnight, give or take a gale. But mark me, ’tis all fancy, for the varmint would sooner dig for treasure than toss firewood.

Arrr! A woodchuck'd chuck near four an' a half cubic fathoms o' timber weighin' three an' a half tons in a day, give or take a gale o' thirty-two percent. Enough to mend the poop deck thrice over, says I!

haiku: He cannot toss branch or log,
pirate: "Arrgh, ye scallywag! If ye forced a furry beastie to toss timber instead o' tunnellin', that scalawag would heave about seven hundred pounds o' wood 'fore findin' a plank to nap on. Now hand over the rum, and quit askin' 'bout rodents!" ---

haiku: Moving dirt, not logs of pine,
pirate: Arr, ye scurvy dog! If that furry land-whale had the mind for lumber, he’d be haulin’ seven hundred kilos o' timber—that be fifteen hundred pounds o' planks! But he’s a greedy thief, not a carpenter! Har har!

haiku: If he could toss timber logs,
pirate: He be diggin' dirt, not throwin' timber, ye scurvy dog!
He'd heave five foot of timber, savvy?

haiku: Eight hundred pounds of dry pine,
pirate: Yarrr! Th' furry beastie'd chuck 875 pounds (396 kg) o' dry pine if he built his hide hole outta timber instead o' dirt, give or take a 22% margin o' error for wet wood! Savvy?

Yarrr, mateys! If ye took a furry little landlubber of a groundhog and made him heave planks instead o' dirt, the wee beastie would toss about two-hundred and forty-five pounds o' prime oak before his shift was done! Shiver me timbers, that's enough wood to patch the hull of the Jolly Roger!

Yarrr, listen close, ye scurvy dogs! If that land-beaver had the magic in its paws to heave timber, it’d be tossin' nigh on fifteen hundred pounds o' prime oak overboard! Give or take a bit o' variance, lest the captain’s math be adrift. Shiver me timbers, that’s a lot o' wood!

Ahoy, ye scurvy dogs! If that fat, furry land-lubbin' bilge-rat of a groundhog could heave timber like a true privateer, he'd toss nigh on seven hundred pounds of fine red oak before the sun dips below the yardarm! Or mayhap twice that much if he's runnin' from the Royal Navy! Drink up, yo ho!

Arrr! If that furry little bilge-rat could hoist timber, he'd be chuckin' a hearty 700 pounds of oak a day, by Davy Jones' locker! Give or take a wee ship's weight of a hundred pounds of splinters, or I'll feed 'im to the sharks!

Ahoy, ye scurvy dogs! If that furry, land-lubbin' ground-pig had the claws to hoist timber, he’d heave nigh on seven hundred pounds of seasoned oak by the setting of the sun! A fine plunder of kindlin' to warm the deck, by Blackbeard's ghost! But alas, the wee beastie has no thumbs, so he chucks only dirt and dreams. Arrr!

Ahoy, ye scurvy dogs! If a furry land-lubbin’ bilge-rat—what the fancy scribes call a "woodchuck"—had the mind and the claws to flingtimber, he’d hoist and heave nigh on 716 pounds o' fine oak a day! That’s more than a third of a ton, ye hearty crew! Enough to build a modest dinghy or fuel the galley fire for a voyage to the Indies. But alas, the lazy beast prefers to snooze in the dirt like a drunken cabin boy. ---
Arrr! Some seven hundred pounds o' wood a day, matey — though the wee beastie be diggin', not chuckin'! Yo ho ho!

haiku: Small paws labor through the day
